summary: Gruber revisits his 2004 prediction that a display-less iPod was implausible, acknowledging he was wrong when Apple released the iPod Shuffle. He reflects on what he missed: Apple's willingness to forgo library navigation entirely in favor of simple shuffle playback. He then proposes that Apple could improve the Shuffle with a text-to-speech audio interface, inspired by Amazon's Kindle text-to-speech feature. He notes the Shuffle hasn't been significantly redesigned since 2006 and argues it's overdue, especially as a recession-friendly product. He updates the post acknowledging that the iPod Nano already has spoken menu features.
key_points: Gruber admits he was wrong in 2004 when he dismissed rumors of a screenless iPod as implausible
His error was assuming Apple would need to provide library navigation — instead Apple simply embraced random shuffle playback
He proposes adding text-to-speech navigation to the Shuffle, inspired by the Kindle's text-to-speech capability
The Shuffle hadn't been significantly redesigned since 2006 and was overdue for innovation
The iPod Nano already had spoken menu features, making the idea even more feasible for the Shuffle
money_quotes: What I overlooked was the possibility that Apple wouldn't even try to provide any real means of navigating your library while on the Shuffle.
It was and remains enough just to shuffle through a pile of songs randomly or, if you prefer, linearly.
Sure, it's "just the Shuffle", but it's bound to be redesigned eventually, and what better product to redesign in this economy than the economy-class iPod?
They can't make it significantly smaller, so why not a text-to-speech interface?
Sign me up for one that offers a voice like HAL's.
I just don't see how you could get an even vaguely decent experience without any display at all.
key_insight: Gruber's failed prediction about the iPod Shuffle illustrates how the most innovative design decisions often succeed by eliminating expected features rather than replacing them.
